summary_sections.title: Section 1: Limits and Continuity
summary_sections.content_html: A limit describes the value that a function approaches as the input (independent variable) approaches a specific value, written as lim(x→a) f(x) = L. This is a fundamental concept in calculus that allows us to analyze function behavior near a point, even when the function is not defined at that point. Importantly, for a limit to exist, the function must approach the same value from both the left side (left-hand limit) and the right side (right-hand limit) as x approaches the value a.

summary_sections.title: Section 2: Limits and Continuity
summary_sections.content_html: The formal ε-δ definition of a limit states that lim(x→a) f(x) = L if for every small positive number ε (epsilon), there exists a positive number δ (delta) such that whenever 0 < |x - a| < δ, we have |f(x) - L| < ε. This rigorous definition ensures that no matter how small an acceptable error bound ε we set, we can always find an interval around a (defined by δ) where all function values stay within that error bound of L. This formal approach is essential for proving limit properties and theorems.

summary_sections.title: Section 3: Limits and Continuity
summary_sections.content_html: A function is continuous at a point a if three conditions are satisfied: (1) f(a) is defined, (2) lim(x→a) f(x) exists, and (3) lim(x→a) f(x) = f(a). In other words, the function must be defined at the point, the limit must exist, and the limit must equal the actual function value at that point. A function is continuous on an interval if it is continuous at every point in that interval, meaning it can be drawn without lifting the pencil from the paper.

summary_sections.title: Section 4: Limits and Continuity
summary_sections.content_html: Different types of discontinuities occur when a function fails to be continuous at a point: a removable discontinuity (or hole) occurs when lim f(x) exists but either f(a) is undefined or does not equal the limit; a jump discontinuity occurs when the left and right limits exist but are not equal; an infinite discontinuity occurs when the function approaches infinity (like at a vertical asymptote). Understanding and identifying these discontinuities is crucial for analyzing function behavior and is foundational for studying derivatives and integrals.

flashcards.question: Define a limit
flashcards.answer: The value a function approaches as the input approaches a specific value.

flashcards.question: What does lim(x→a) f(x) = L mean?
flashcards.answer: As x approaches a, the function values f(x) approach L.

flashcards.question: What are the three conditions for continuity at x = a?
flashcards.answer: f(a) is defined; lim f(x) as x→a exists; lim f(x) = f(a).

flashcards.question: What is a removable discontinuity?
flashcards.answer: A hole in the graph where the limit exists but f(a) is undefined or does not equal the limit.

flashcards.question: What is a jump discontinuity?
flashcards.answer: A point where the left-hand and right-hand limits exist but are not equal.

flashcards.question: What is an infinite discontinuity?
flashcards.answer: A discontinuity where the function approaches infinity, usually at a vertical asymptote.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between left-hand and right-hand limits?
flashcards.answer: Left-hand limit: approaching from values less than a; Right-hand limit: approaching from values greater than a.

flashcards.question: Can a function be continuous at a point where it is not defined?
flashcards.answer: No. For continuity, the function must be defined at the point (f(a) must exist).

flashcards.question: What does the ε-δ definition of a limit require?
flashcards.answer: For any ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that 0 < |x - a| < δ implies |f(x) - L| < ε.

flashcards.question: If the left and right limits are equal, what can we conclude?
flashcards.answer: The limit exists and equals the common value of the left and right limits.

quiz_questions.question_text: For lim(x→a) f(x) to equal L, what must be true?

options.text: The left and right limits must be equal

quiz_questions.question_text: A removable discontinuity can be eliminated by...

options.text: Redefining f(a) to equal the limit

quiz_questions.question_text: At a jump discontinuity...

options.text: The left and right limits are not equal

quiz_questions.question_text: A function is continuous on an interval if...

options.text: It is continuous at every point in the interval
It is defined at every point
It has a limit at every point

quiz_questions.question_text: Which type of discontinuity occurs at a vertical asymptote?
If lim(x→2) f(x) = 5 and f(2) = 5, then...

options.text: The function has a jump discontinuity
The function is continuous at x = 2
The function has a removable discontinuity

quiz_questions.question_text: The difference between lim(x→3⁻) f(x) and lim(x→3⁺) f(x) tells us...

options.text: The slope of the tangent line
